speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gun, can you tell us who we have here today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To my far right is my granddaughter, and only granddaughter, Laurel Ruza. Next to her is my lovely wife Miryam-- which, we've been married for 37 years. And plugged along with me to help me thaw out from the Holocaust. And next to me is my oldest grandson, Matthew Ruza. And next to him is the oldest-- my oldest grandson from my son, Max Gun. And to my far right, standing, is my daughter-in-law, Susan. And in between is my grandson Josh and my son, Sam. And next to her-- next to him is my daughter, Sandra. And next to her is my favorite son-in-law, TJ Ruza.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything that you want to tell your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to tell them I'm very, very proud of them all. This is the-- I'm in-- in this stage of my life, this is the, the most that I enjoy. Never was able to enjoy my kids that much when I was younger. I used to work long hours. And now I get a lot of enjoyment out of seeing their accomplishments and being with my grandchildren. I love to spend time with them. I-- the oldest one became a baseball fan like his grandpa. I play baseball with him, take him to the baseball games. And I'm looking forward to do the same with Max and Joshua, hopefully.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, we actually didn't cover this before, but what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy!

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you work-- you used to work long hours. What, what kind of industry did you work in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was in the meat industry. I used to have a, a meat store for about four years and used to work long hours, weekends, come home 9 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy!

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- and then I had a little sausage manufacturing company, which also required long--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy!

speaker: interviewee
text: Max, you want me to pick-- you want to stand?

words.text: want me to pick-- you want to stand?

speaker: interviewee
text: --also required long hours. And, uh, didn't get much time to spend with the kids when they were growing up. So I really enjoy now the time I spend babysitting or playing with them. And-- that's really, this is the-- this is all. This is-- like I said before, I'm not working anymore. And this is my-- this is my enjoyment now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a question for the kids, anybody who'd like to answer. Did what your father went through, his experiences-- did it affect you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I'm gonna answer that one. I guess, in a way, no. It, it did, but my dad didn't really talk about it that much. And I think he didn't talk about it, not that he didn't want us to know, but more so because he didn't want us to be burdened with what he went through. And more recently, when we started talking about it, I think it-- telling his story kind of made us more aware of what-- of what he went through and made us more proud of, of what he went through and proud of him. And I think, because of that, it's, it's made us a closer, more tighter family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any thoughts on when you want to tell your children about [BACKROUND NOISE] what their grandfather went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's a good question. I'm not sure. I certainly would like them--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --to be old enough to understand it. [INTERPOSING VOICES] I think even something like this would be a good thing for them to watch beforehand as a preface. So one day I can explain to them, and they'll have more of an understanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so grateful that our dad has done this, because we have exactly what went on to share with our children. I mean, we could preface it a little bit, but we can sit down as a family and watch the tape together. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we can-- we can stop it and ask questions, and it's just a full, illustrated testimonial to what he went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any-- anybody who would like to say anything to Mr. Gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll say something. I, I, I really think that my father-in-law needs to be commended, for taking the time to do this video. As my brother-in-law and my wife have said, I think it gives the family an opportunity to hear the story directly from him. This has, this has been a very difficult thing for him to do as well as a, a horrendous experience for anyone. And I think that through this video, as well as his work with the Holocaust Center and with the Hidden Children, it really has helped him, as well as helped the family. And I will reiterate what my brother-in-law said. I think that my father-in-law tried to protect his children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in knowing what really went on. And I think he really should be commended for doing something like this. You know, I think that this is an opportunity for all of us to tell my father-in-law how much we love him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and how much we all admire him and what strength, courage, persistence, as well as the terrific sense of humor that he has. And he really is the matriarch of the family here. And he is somebody to look up to, to be admired. And he is a tremendous role model for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our children.

speaker: interviewee
text: --our children, his grandchildren, as well as his own children. I've been around in this family for a long time, almost over 20 years already. So I have a lot of experience and have shared a lot of good times and bad times with the whole family. And so has Sue, my sister-in-law. She's been around, also, for a long time here, too. And I think that what he's gone through, as well as the whole family, is a horrible experience. But I think that it's important that my children and his children hear, because it also tells them their history. And I think the other thing that should be remembered is that there are aunts, there are uncles, there are grandparents that should be remembered. And this is a way for them to know and for them to hear their heritage. And we're very proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'm proud of you, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Gun, any thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't-- I'm not much of a speaker, so I just-- I don't know. I-- I'm just-- I have such a beautiful family, and I don't have too much to say, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anybody have any final thoughts or comments?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Matthew, you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matthew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my papa. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's one of your best buddies, isn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you say that, Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, can you say you love your papa? Do you love Papa? Do you? Tell the camera. See the camera over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you say, I love my papa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say, I love you, Papa.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, Papa.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about Laurel? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Laurel, what are you going to say?

words.text: what are you going to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wanna say, I love my papa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my papa!

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to say something by closing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I hope and pray that my children or my grandchildren never experience anything that I did. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also hope that they will teach their children to see, to make them aware, as I said before-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --make them aware of what tragedies can happen from hatred and racism. And to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --and to teach it to not only to Jewish people, but to the whole world as a whole, to see that some things that what happened during the Hitler regime will never happen again to any nation or any creed or any nationality. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the conclusion of the videotaping session. We used six tapes.
